region_of_origin: Tropical Americas; widely cultivated in Southeast Asia and Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn entry covers sweet potato broadly; boiled and peeled preparation specificity not captured at this level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers boiled sweet potato; orange flesh variety not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT code identified for boiled orange sweet potato without skin; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one medium boiled orange sweet potato piece of approximately 150 g, suitable as a side dish or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet Potato, Boiled — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled sweet potato, orange, peeled. All core macros and micronutrients reflect HPB-reported per-100g values.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Sweet Potato, boiled
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 61 (medium) applied from University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled sweet potato. Orange-flesh variety is not separately indexed; this value represents the closest defensible analogous match for boiled sweet potato. Glycemic load calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 x 15.2 x 1.5) / 100 = 13.9 for a 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (10 mg/100g) and high potassium (242 mg/100g) supporting DASH dietary goals
hypertension.guidance: Boiled orange sweet potato is an excellent choice for hypertension management due to its very low sodium content (10 mg/100g) and meaningful potassium (242 mg/100g), which together support healthy blood pressure; avoid adding salt or butter post-cooking to preserve this favourable profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (61) with moderate carbohydrate content (15.2 g/100g) moderated by dietary fibre (2.7 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled orange sweet potato has a medium GI of 61 and provides 15.2 g carbohydrate per 100g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–150 g per meal, pair with protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and avoid preparations that raise GI such as mashing with added sugar or butter.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.1 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and meaningful dietary fibre (2.7 g/100g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled orange sweet potato is cardioprotective due to its negligible fat (0.1 g/100g), zero cholesterol, low sodium (10 mg/100g), and 2.7 g dietary fibre per 100g, which contributes to LDL reduction; it is suitable as a regular component of a heart-healthy diet when prepared without added fats or salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled sweet potato, orange, peeled is a minimally processed starchy vegetable prepared by boiling orange-fleshed sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) and removing the skin before or after cooking. The result is a soft, tender, naturally sweet food with a moist, slightly fibrous texture and a warm orange colour derived from high beta-carotene content. It is widely consumed across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, as a snack, side dish, or dessert component. Per 100g it provides 75 kcal, 2.0g protein, 0.1g fat, 15.2g carbohydrate, 6.0g natural sugars, 2.7g dietary fibre, 10mg sodium, and 242mg potassium. The medium glycemic index (GI 61) means blood glucose impact is moderate and is further buffered by the intact food matrix and fibre content. Clinically, it is suitable for hypertension management due to its low sodium and good potassium, and is appropriate for cardiovascular health given its negligible fat and zero cholesterol. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume controlled portions of 100–150g per meal. The orange flesh is a rich source of beta-carotene (pro-vitamin A) and antioxidants relevant to reducing oxidative stress. Sweet potato is not a nightshade vegetable and contains no solanine.
common_user_queries: Is boiled sweet potato good for diabetes?
How many calories in boiled sweet potato?
Is orange sweet potato high in sugar?
Can I eat sweet potato if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of boiled sweet potato?
Is sweet potato good for heart health?
Is boiled sweet potato vegan and gluten free?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate for boiled sweet potato. All mandatory fields populated from approved sources.
notes: saturated_fat_g reported as 0.0: confirmed negligible per HPB source for this preparation; fat_g of 0.1g is naturally present as trace lipid in the vegetable matrix. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full vitamin panel, and moisture data not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this specific food item as of April 2026; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy #11508 Sweet potato, cooked, boiled, without skin) provides partial reference but was not used as primary source to maintain HPB primacy for Singapore records. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled sweet potato; orange-flesh variety not separately indexed. Glycemic load calculated for 150g serving: (61 x 15.2 x 1.5) / 100 = 13.9. The input record contained a contradictory GI note citing ~44-46 in the health_suitability section; the University of Sydney GI Database value of 61 (medium) has been adopted as the approved-source value. Sweet potato is not a nightshade (Solanaceae) plant; no solanine concern applies.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name present. Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names all populated. Food is a generic starchy vegetable; no Singaporean dish multilingual gate applies but local names provided for completeness given HPB Singapore provenance.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (75.0), protein_g (2.0), fat_g (0.1), carbohydrate_g (15.2) all present and numeric. Source is HPB Singapore Foodo, an approved primary regional source. nutrient_source fully populated.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (2.7), sodium_mg (10.0), saturated_fat_g (0.0), sugar_g (6.0) all present as numeric values. saturated_fat_g confirmed negligible per HPB source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (61), gi_category (medium), glycemic_load_per_serving (13.9), and gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate for boiled sweet potato with full documentation.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided with close match and gap note. FoodEx2 code A00LC provided with close match and gap note. SNOMED CT left null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and meal_context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern), Cardiovascular Disease (low concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for diabetes is Type 2 Diabetes; machine key is type2_diabetes.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains all strings from aliases_by_language. English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi aliases present. Alternate English names, romanized forms, and local-language forms all captured. Self-check passed: every aliases_by_language string appears verbatim in flat aliases array.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all six hard gates pass and all eight Q criteria score 1/1. Core macros complete from HPB Singapore approved source. GI from University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology honest with gap notes. Multilingual names present for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. Type 2 Diabetes health context present with correct machine key. Aliases consistent between flat array and structured object. LLM description substantive and clinically relevant.
